title: Real-time Data Sensor Calibration | University of Nottinghamn - School of Computer Science
excerpt: A second-year Software Engineering group project carried out throughout the full academic year 25/26, supervised by Lucas Fonseca.
content:
## Project Overview 📝

As part of the software engineering group project module at the University of Nottingham that ran across the full academic year, my team of 6 applied to three different projects, went through interviews, and ended up being the top applicant team for this project amongst several competing teams.

Real-Time Sensor Calibration (RTSC) was honestly one of the best projects we could have worked on. It brought together both software and hardware in a way that made the project feel truly real and meaningful. What started as an academic project slowly turned into something bigger — a system with real potential to continue being used and developed in future robotics research.

Below, you’ll find a more detailed overview of the project, my involvement throughout the year, and my reflections at the end of this journey :)

---

## EMG Background & Problem ⛔️
EMG sensors detect the electrical activity produced by muscles during contraction. However, the raw signal produced is very noisy and differs from one person to another due to factors such as electrode placement, surrounding electrical interference, and natural differences in muscle activity, which makes it inconsistent to use directly.

---

## Hardware Components
The project was originally intended to use research-grade Delsys EMG sensors for real-time robotic interaction and control. However, because access to the laboratory and equipment was limited throughout development, a large part of the project ended up being developed using the MyoWare 2.0 EMG muscle sensor instead.

The main hardware used throughout the project included:

- MyoWare 2.0 EMG Muscle Sensor connected with an ESP32 module to allow continuous EMG acquisition through a Bluetooth connection. Due to its accessibility and portability, this setup became the primary hardware used throughout most of the software development and testing process.
![small|right|soft](data:image/jpeg;base64,)

![small|left|soft](data:image/jpeg;base64,)
- Delsys EMG Sensors, which were intended for the research-focused side of the project and integration with the Kinova Gen3 robotic arm for real-time muscle-based robotic control.
![small|right|soft](data:image/jpeg;base64,)

![small|left|soft](data:image/jpeg;base64,)
Both sensor systems were used for calibration workflows, live signal processing, JSON profile generation, and frontend interaction within the software.

---

## System Architecture 🏛️

The RTSC system architecture is built around real-time acquisition, processing, calibration, and visualisation of EMG sensor data. Live muscle signals are collected from the EMG sensor or replayed from recorded CSV samples, then processed through filtering and smoothing stages before being stored in a real-time buffer.

The backend manages sensor communication, calibration workflows, data processing, and reusable calibration profiles, while WebSocket streaming enables low-latency real-time communication with the frontend dashboard. The frontend provides live visualisation through interactive graphs, contraction feedback bars, calibration controls, and system monitoring tools, allowing users to interact with their muscle activity in real time.
![full|center|soft](data:image/jpeg;base64,)
---

## Calibration & Signal Processing Pipeline

The calibration and signal processing side of the project was mainly focused on turning noisy raw EMG signals into something reliable to be reused in real time. During calibration, users went through guided contraction stages where multiple relax, medium, and maximum contractions were recorded to better understand each person’s muscle activation range. From these recordings, the system generated hysteresis activation bands with ±10% margins that were later saved into reusable JSON calibration profiles.

The recorded EMG data then passed through filtering, smoothing, and normalisation stages to reduce noise and make the live interaction more stable. One thing we quickly realised throughout development was that the MyoWare sensor struggled to consistently separate medium and maximum contractions, which made the calibration workflow less reliable. Because of that, the medium stage was eventually removed to simplify the experience and improve calibration consistency overall.

A large part of the project also focused on keeping the interaction feeling genuinely real time. From the moment a muscle signal was captured to the moment it appeared visually on the frontend, the system usually stayed between 13 ms and 16 ms of total latency, which comfortably stayed below the original 50 ms requirement while still maintaining smooth live interaction and visual feedback.
![full|center|soft](data:image/jpeg;base64,)
---

## Frontend & User Interaction 👥

The frontend was developed using React and TypeScript with a strong focus on interactivity and user experience. A major part of the project was being able to calibrate and visualise live EMG signals in real time, which made the live graph one of the main elements and core focuses of the whole system. To keep the dashboard clean while still giving easy access to controls and features, different settings and tools were organised through the navigation bar located on the top left of the interface.

The calibration workflow was designed to feel guided and simple to follow, with audio SFX and live visual feedback helping users interact with the system more naturally during the calibration stages. For the final demo day, a small emulator-style game was also integrated into the software so people could interact with the system in a more engaging and fun way using their own muscle activity.

At the bottom of the dashboard, live system information and diagnostics were continuously displayed, including connection states, stream rate, backend rate, latency measurements, Band WebSocket status, and backend status. This helped monitor the overall performance and stability of the system in real time during both development and demonstrations.

---

## Technical Challenges 🚨

- Noisy and inconsistent EMG signals caused by electrode placement, muscle differences, movement, and surrounding electrical interference.
- Designing a reliable calibration workflow that could adapt to different users while keeping the system stable and responsive.
- Creating hysteresis activation bands to reduce rapid fluctuations and false triggering during live interaction.
- Balancing signal responsiveness and stability so the system reacted quickly without producing unstable or flickering outputs.
- Maintaining low-latency real-time streaming and visualisation, with end-to-end latency typically remaining between 13 ms and 16 ms, comfortably below the 50 ms project requirement.
- Handling continuous live updates at 60 Hz without causing frontend lag or unstable graph rendering.
- Managing real-time synchronisation between live sensor acquisition, backend processing, WebSocket communication, and frontend rendering.
- Bluetooth connectivity instability and sensor disconnections during long continuous streaming sessions.
- Preventing unnecessary frontend rerenders and performance drops during continuous real-time graph updates.
- Designing a modular architecture where live sensors, replay systems, calibration workflows, backend processing, and frontend interaction could operate independently without affecting the stability of the rest of the system.
- Building a system flexible enough to support both lightweight consumer EMG hardware and research-grade sensor setups.

---

## My Role Within the Project 👩‍💻

I was elected to be the team administrator, which gave me the responsibility of organising meetings, taking attendance, handling submissions and deadlines, and maintaining communication between the supervisor and the team. I also set up collaboration platforms such as Discord to improve communication and coordination throughout development.

Alongside that, I initially started working on the frontend and built the first simple prototype of the interface before it later evolved into the final real-time dashboard and visualisation system. I also contributed to parts of the backend, including the file manager system.

During the second semester, I took the MyoWare sensor home with me over the holidays to properly begin testing whether what we had built so far was fully functional in real conditions outside the laboratory environment. A large part of my contribution later focused on frontend interaction, calibration behaviour, latency monitoring, and improving the overall live user experience of the system.
![full|center|soft](data:image/jpeg;base64,)
Some of the main issues and improvements I worked on included:

| Issue Identified                                                                                                                           | Solution / Improvement                                                                                                                                                                            |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Bluetooth streaming stability issues where the connection consistently dropped after around 30 seconds of continuous streaming             | Resolved by throttling excessive BLE notification flooding on the ESP32 side, improving desktop client stability while maintaining real-time streaming performance.                               |
| The live graph and FlexBar occasionally displayed high or maximum activation immediately after connection, even while the user was relaxed | Refined the calibration baseline, activation range, and threshold behaviour to produce more stable live readings and interaction behaviour.                                                       |
| Misleading latency measurements                                                                                                            | Split displayed latency into backend latency and real stream latency instead of relying only on backend ping values.                                                                              |
| FlexBar behaving like a raw signal bar                                                                                                     | Refactored the FlexBar to act as a calibrated activation display using the user’s saved MVC and relax calibration values instead of raw EMG values.                                               |
| Calibration workflow complexity                                                                                                            | Removed the medium calibration stage since the MyoWare sensor struggled to consistently distinguish between medium and maximum contractions, making the workflow more reliable and user-friendly. |
| Missing relax baseline data affecting calibration accuracy                                                                                 | Fixed calibration handling so relax baseline values were correctly included within saved calibration results.                                                                                     |
| Hysteresis band calculation instability                                                                                                    | Refined hysteresis band generation to use contraction stage averages and activation ranges instead of unstable max-only calculations.                                                             |
| Calibration values occasionally being shared across users                                                                                  | Corrected user-specific calibration loading and storage behaviour to isolate profiles properly.                                                                                                   |
| Latency and stale sensor updates affecting live visualisation                                                                              | Reduced unnecessary rebroadcasting of stale sensor samples to improve graph responsiveness and streaming stability.                                                                               |
| Graph display and live signal behaviour becoming unstable during streaming                                                                 | Improved live graph rendering, scaling behaviour, and frontend interaction to maintain smoother real-time visualisation.                                                                          |


---

## After 8 Months of RTSC 💗

As much as this project was time-consuming, all the development, knowledge, and personal growth I gained throughout it feels priceless. It taught me how even the smallest changes in a real-time system can affect the entire logic and behaviour of the software.

The project also taught me the responsibility of always staying on top of things to make sure we delivered work on time while maintaining a high standard throughout development. Working on a system that combined hardware, backend processing, frontend interaction, and real-time streaming made me grow technically far more than I expected at the beginning of the year.

I also discovered something important about myself during this project: I genuinely like my work to feel unique. Whenever I felt something was missing, I could not leave it unfinished until I found a way to improve or refine it. As normal as I once thought my creative side was, the feedback and comments I received from my teammates throughout the project made me realise that I do have my own unique way of presenting ideas, interactions, and user experiences.

Looking back now, RTSC became much more than just a university project. It was one of the first projects that truly made me feel what real software engineering, collaboration, debugging, creativity, and problem-solving look like in practice.

I would also like to thank our supervisor, Lucas Fonseca, who genuinely made this project experience even better. His support, feedback, and encouragement throughout the year helped the team constantly improve and push the project further.

---

## Demo Day & Showcase 👾🦾

As part of the module, we had to present our final software and aim to leave a strong impression on all visitors, including academics, sponsors, supervisors, and students.

Our final demonstration almost felt like two different projects combined into one system due to the different hardware setups and interactions we showcased:

- The MyoWare setup was connected to an integrated emulator where muscle contractions controlled a classic dinosaur-style game. Reaching a specific activation threshold triggered a keyboard spacebar press, while relaxing below another threshold released the input, allowing users to control the game entirely through muscle activity.
[video:short](https://youtube.com/shorts/skcaQnN4n3Y?si=Gx3DH650KSqPxBLz)
- The Delsys setup was demonstrated alongside the Kinova Gen3 robotic arm. Different contraction bands controlled the robot gripper in real time: relaxing opened the gripper, medium contraction held the object, and maximum contraction closed the gripper. This allowed the robot to pick up a cap, move it across an obstacle , and place it into another location through EMG-controlled interaction.
[video:short](https://youtube.com/shorts/6Us6lDBx-PE?si=tFpgoKCflhTfkr74)
One of the most rewarding parts of the showcase was seeing people interact with the system in real time and realise that the project combined live signal processing, calibration, robotics, frontend interaction, and hardware control into one fully working system!

title: AI Is Starting to Move Into the Physical World
excerpt: Recent robotics news shows AI moving beyond screens and into machines that can learn, move, and act in real spaces.
content:
## Why this caught my attention
A lot of AI news still focuses on chatbots, image generators, and software agents. But one of the most interesting shifts happening right now is that AI is starting to move into the physical world.

---

## From language to action
Recent robotics news makes that clear. Meta has acquired a humanoid robotics startup to strengthen its work on AI for robots, while Boston Dynamics is working with Google DeepMind on the next generation of Atlas. The interesting part is not only the hardware. It is the idea that robots may need foundation models of their own: systems trained to understand movement, objects, space, and physical interaction.

> Intelligence becomes more than prediction. It becomes action.

That changes how I think about AI. A chatbot can be wrong and correct itself in text, but a robot acting in the real world has to deal with gravity, friction, timing, safety, and uncertainty. Intelligence becomes more than prediction. It becomes action.

## Why robotics feels exciting
This is why robotics feels like one of the most exciting areas of AI. It combines perception, language, control, planning, and real-world feedback. It also shows how difficult "general intelligence" really is. Understanding a sentence is one challenge. Understanding how to pick up a fragile object, navigate a room, or work safely near people is another.

For me, this is the kind of AI progress worth watching: not just models that can answer questions, but systems that can eventually help in homes, hospitals, factories, and everyday environments.
